text: The massive mass of slow moving friction is un compressible to us. Wind heat cold friction is so big we cannot comprehend it. author: @jessewintersteen4740
text: How does a million pound cumulus cloud float through our atmosphere. its friction and magnetism. When the friction becomes to much you get a static charge which we call lightning. When it releases it charge it releases it magnetism and the rain drops fall. Or the dry lightning clouds seperate. The fuel is heat from the sun and earth which creates friction in the coolness of space. That fuel start the cycle of magnetism. Which is the water cycle. It friction based.

author: @jessewintersteen4740
text: Static electric is really magnetic we do not understand all the aspects of magnetism. Gravity is a form magnetism. If you could figure out how to reverse it you would float away from gravity. Static and friction is a form of magnetism.

author: @RyanLebeck-td5ft
text:
Query: you have had a goal of establishing a system that can scavenge electricity from low wind speeds in urban areas. If you installed a system like that on the wall of a building what would the profile be and could you stretch it along the entire side of the building as multiple segment units? 

I feel that would help you toward your goal of building mounted wind energy scavengers for cities.

author: @michaelkolanda8712
text: I think that some semi rigid teng shingles could both collect electricity from the rain and wind. Could be fairly easily scalable, and could still make it possible to use under a rooftop solar array (although being somewhat limited physically). It would be even better if these were made using recycled plastics.

author: @PerilousPaddy
text: Living in Cornwall but in a rather heavily populated zone we could use wind but only if it isn't noisy, the flapping flag style might work because as the wind blows loudly it should mask the generators.  I am going to be working on a wind wall railing using 3d printable HAWT's that you showed us not long ago for the garden path and would love to have a Cornish flag covering a flappy generation device on a flag pole at the end of the garden right next to the public footpath.  Combining those two with some water collectors (either condensation from the air or just filtered) for drinking water would reduce our living costs quite dramatically I think.

text: Build a WINDhurst generator! Haha, high voltage,  charge a capacitor bank!,with a bank of wilmhurst  . Build a tower with the wilmhurst on their side under the tower.  With a shaft from a vertical axis turbine. Build a  cap  bank to clamp the voltage to something useful, using DC converter to charge a battery bank from it. A stavk of 36" wilmhurst about 12' tall it may get close to a couple amps at 96v, just as well toss some magnets on a disc with coils to take advantage of fast wind,and the wilmhurs for slow wind.  If it can be built cheap it may be worth building??....

author: @overunity_inventor
text:
Tom bearden once said in an interview that if you connect an AC source to a step up transformer and connect capacitors in series to both ends of secondary of that step up transformer, and then connect the other ends of both the capacitors to another step up transformer and connect secondary of this 2nd step up transformer to loads in parallel. 

Now when you add load to secondary of 2nd transformer the first transformer will not draw more current from AC source. The load runs from displacement current. 

Can u try doing it please and share with your audience the results of this experiment?

Thanks Robert!

author: @gerryjamesedwards1227
text: One of the people who watch the goings on with SpaceX spotted those little antennae that jets use to dissipate charge that builds up on surfaces in supersonic winds, on the trailing edges of the Starship's fins. It would be interesting to see whether a system,  or material, could be developed that would generate charge in the same manner but at lower wind-speeds.
